name: tefnut (moisture) - his first creations
shortDescription: Tefnut is the Egyptian goddess of moisture, rain, and fertility. She is one of the first deities created by Atum (or Ra in some versions).
description: Tefnut is a prominent figure in Egyptian mythology, embodying moisture, water, and fertility. She is typically depicted as a woman with the head of a lioness, symbolizing her fierce and protective nature. As one of the first deities created by Atum (or Ra, depending on the cosmogony), usually through spitting or sneezing, Tefnut is vital to the creation and maintenance of the world. Her union with Shu, the god of air, produced Geb (earth) and Nut (sky), further establishing her as a crucial ancestor of the Egyptian pantheon. Tefnut's absence from the world brought drought and hardship, highlighting the importance of moisture for life and prosperity. Myths often depict her returning from a distant land, bringing much-needed rain and restoring balance to the cosmos. Her role as a bringer of life-giving water connects her to themes of creation, order (Ma'at), and power within the Egyptian religious system. She helps to maintain the proper order of the world by providing the moisture necessary for life to flourish. Tefnut's connection to the lioness also links her to royal power and protection, further solidifying her important position among the Egyptian gods.

sources.title: The Complete Gods and Goddesses of Ancient Egypt
sources.author: Richard H. Wilkinson

sources.title: Egyptian Mythology: A Guide to the Gods, Goddesses, and Traditions of Ancient Egypt
sources.author: Geraldine Pinch
